story:
The ground opens beneath Liora before she can scream, and she drops into a narrow stone shaft that feels like a well without a bucket, a throat without a voice. Stone presses cold against her palms; moss-slick walls rise to a distant circle of sky. When she calls for Kai, her voice comes back thinner, swallowed by the dark. He will come, she tells herself. He always does. But this darkness isn’t the tunneled kind she grew up with; it holds no passages, no direction, only itself. The air smells of old earth and damp. The memory of sun on her eyelids, wind against her skin—new to her and already precious—slips away.

She has lived underground all her life and never questioned it, until coming above made space feel like breath. Now the thought of being buried makes her chest tighten. She keeps calling, voice frayed. Unless, she thinks, ghosts can’t go underground. The thought drops through her like a stone. If he can’t reach her, if he can’t even hear her, the dark might be forever. She presses her forehead to wet rock, tears stinging where they scrape her skin, grounding her in the hurt of being alive.

A faint shimmer in the ankle-deep water draws her eye. She reaches into the cold and pulls up a dagger, old and blackened but edged with a dull gleam. Under the grime, the hilt is etched with a pattern she knows: the spiral-edged mark that curls around Kai’s wrist. He once said it was older than him. Now it hums faintly in her palm—or maybe it’s her pulse. The well seems to change around that sound: whispers that might be water, echoes that might be her own breath. She whispers his name. The dark answers with drips and hush. For a heartbeat she thinks she hears him, but when she listens, there is no one. The truth arrives quietly: Kai isn’t coming.

Air feels thinner. Light shrinks. If no one can save her, she will have to climb. She tests the wall for holds. The dagger’s weight pulls in her hand. She almost leaves it, then tucks it close and starts up. Fear burns away into something harder. Up, up, up, her heartbeat says. The wall is slick; her fingers slip; her boots scrape uselessly. She falls. The cold knocks breath from her and leaves her under the halo of sky, reaching as if she could hold the light in her hands. No. Not here. Not like this.

She uses the dagger like a piton, wedging its tip into cracks, hauling herself higher. The metal hums as she moves; the light grows. A stone shifts; she lurches; instinct drags the blade hard along the wall to slow her, pain sparking up her arm. She catches, braces, climbs again. The world can bury her, but she will not stay buried. She keeps going until her fingers hook the rim. She scrapes, trembles, and finally hauls herself onto grass. Night air cuts cool across her face. She lies there, gasping, the earth steady under her.

“About time,” a voice says. Kai stands a few feet away, ghostlight soft around him, arms crossed, smirk tugging his mouth. She laughs, shaky. He could have helped. He says he wouldn’t ruin her dramatic escape. The dagger hums beside her. Its mark binds it to him, and now to her. She doesn’t know yet what it means, only that she is alive, and the sky is real.
